conclusion: Ellenbogen hunts for the tiny 1% of public companies that compound wealth for years. His answer to machine-driven markets is not faster trading. It is owning fewer businesses with deep change insight, then using forced volatility to buy more.
background: Henry Ellenbogen built his philosophy by studying long-run public market winners and the mistakes of selling too early. He argues that over rolling 10-year periods, about 40 stocks out of roughly 4,000 public companies compound at 20% a year and produce more than 6x returns. The conversation covers Walmart, Domino's, Duolingo, Figma, DoorDash, Affirm, Toast, Colliers, Workday, Shopify, AI's impact on old-economy leaders, and why human judgment still matters when machines dominate short-term signals.
important_facts: Ellenbogen says roughly 40 of 4,000 public stocks over a rolling 10-year period compound wealth at ≈20% per year, or a little over 6x; about 80% of those companies start their compounding journey as small caps. Durable usually has 10-15% of capital in private markets and the rest in public markets; Ellenbogen says he has invested in 100-plus private companies, been involved in 50-plus IPOs, and still owns names like DoorDash, Affirm, and Toast.

He cites a retail fund of ≈$8B where selling Walmart after the IPO road show overwhelmed many other good decisions; Walmart came public with only ≈50 stores. Key quantitative evidence: Ellenbogen says roughly 40 of 4,000 public stocks over a rolling 10-year period compound wealth at ≈20% per year, or a little over 6x; about 80% of those companies start their compounding journey as small caps.

Durable usually has 10-15% of capital in private markets and the rest in public markets; Ellenbogen says he has invested in 100-plus private companies, been involved in 50-plus IPOs, and still owns names like DoorDash, Affirm, and Toast. IWO (Watch) — Ellenbogen says roughly 80% of long-run public compounders start as small caps, making Russell 2000 Growth exposure interesting while reinforcing that security selection matters more than the factor alone.

mental_model.name: Do Less So You Can Do More
mental_model.components: The market's biggest winners are rare enough to justify obsession. If only about 1% of public stocks create the exceptional compounding, the job is not broad diversification. It is maximizing the odds of identifying and holding the 40 companies that matter over each decade. Machines win short-term repeatable patterns; humans still win change and people judgment. Ellenbogen says quants are better when the problem is known data and repeat behavior. Durable doubles down where the data is messy: whether a founder can do an act two, whether a culture can allocate capital, and whether AI changes a physical-world business. Market structure creates opportunity by shortening others' horizons. He estimates 80-90% of institutional flow is driven by one-month/three-month pod incentives or quants reacting to price signals. That can make earnings seasons more volatile than fundamentals justify and create chances for owners with longer horizons.
mental_model.application: Ellenbogen hunts for the tiny 1% of public companies that compound wealth for years. His answer to machine-driven markets is not faster trading. It is owning fewer businesses with deep change insight, then using forced volatility to buy more. Duolingo is used as an example of a private-to-public compounder where Durable would add after volatility if founder quality, product improvement, and market expansion remain intact DoorDash is named as one of Durable's large public positions that began as a late private-round investment and fits the public-private compounding playbook Ellenbogen says roughly 80% of long-run public compounders start as small caps, making Russell 2000 Growth exposure interesting while reinforcing that security selection matters more than the factor alone Ellenbogen says roughly 40 of 4,000 public stocks over a rolling 10-year period compound wealth at ≈20% per year, or a little over 6x; about 80% of those companies start their compounding journey as small caps. He cites a retail fund of ≈$8B where selling Walmart after the IPO road show overwhelmed many other good decisions; Walmart came public with only ≈50 stores.

key_insights.view: The market's biggest winners are rare enough to justify obsession.
key_insights.question: Why focus so narrowly?
key_insights.answer: If only about 1% of public stocks create the exceptional compounding, the job is not broad diversification. It is maximizing the odds of identifying and holding the 40 companies that matter over each decade. Ellenbogen says roughly 40 of 4,000 public stocks over a rolling 10-year period compound wealth at ≈20% per year, or a little over 6x; about 80% of those companies start their compounding journey as small caps.

key_insights.view: Machines win short-term repeatable patterns; humans still win change and people judgment.
key_insights.question: Where is the human edge?
key_insights.answer: Ellenbogen says quants are better when the problem is known data and repeat behavior. Durable doubles down where the data is messy: whether a founder can do an act two, whether a culture can allocate capital, and whether AI changes a physical-world business. He cites a retail fund of ≈$8B where selling Walmart after the IPO road show overwhelmed many other good decisions; Walmart came public with only ≈50 stores.

key_insights.question: How do pod funds change the game?
key_insights.answer: He estimates 80-90% of institutional flow is driven by one-month/three-month pod incentives or quants reacting to price signals. That can make earnings seasons more volatile than fundamentals justify and create chances for owners with longer horizons. Durable usually has 10-15% of capital in private markets and the rest in public markets; Ellenbogen says he has invested in 100-plus private companies, been involved in 50-plus IPOs, and still owns names like DoorDash, Affirm, and Toast.

top_investment_implications.thesis: Duolingo is used as an example of a private-to-public compounder where Durable would add after volatility if founder quality, product improvement, and market expansion remain intact
DoorDash is named as one of Durable's large public positions that began as a late private-round investment and fits the public-private compounding playbook

Ellenbogen says roughly 80% of long-run public compounders start as small caps, making Russell 2000 Growth exposure interesting while reinforcing that security selection matters more than the factor alone

golden_quotes: Ellenbogen thinks the short-term alpha game will probably be won by machines paired with humans, so Durable avoids competing there.
His operating rule for Durable is to do less so the team can go deeper on the rare companies that can compound for a decade.
He warns that an investor cannot honestly claim a time horizon longer than their career horizon.

events.event: Walmart comes public with about 50 stores, becoming the kind of compounding winner he later studies
He concludes roughly 20 stocks drove a major fund's long-run performance
Invests across private and public growth companies, including names that later IPO
Many high-growth companies come public in the zero-rate IPO window
Q2 earnings volatility is described as the highest since the financial crisis despite less fundamental stress
